comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around a game simulating life randomness, with users sharing experiences and insights about probabilities and biological facts. Some comments provide thoughtful observations about life chances and species prevalence, while others are brief or humorous.

comments.author: breppp
comments.text:
There was a game using this mechanic called RealLives https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Real_Lives_(video_game)

You had a random chance at being born anywhere in the world weighted by population. A big lesson from the game is if you are born in Africa and you survive childhood, your best bet is somehow immigrating to the West
comments.analysis: This comment provides a relevant and accurate reference to a similar game and offers a realistic social insight about survival and migration, making it highly informative.

comments.author: dash2
comments.text:
I got 15 nematodes in a row which seems a lot even for an 80% chance.

Update: so now I learned something about compounding as well as about nematodes. Prob is about 0.03, much more than I’d have guessed.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects a thoughtful engagement with probability and biology, showing learning and analysis, which adds depth to the discussion.

comments.author: al_borland
comments.text: I was not aware 95% of all life was nematodes and beetles.
comments.analysis: This comment states a biological fact that is broadly accurate, contributing useful context about species abundance.

comments.author: soulchild37
comments.text: The random seed seems fixed, I tried 100 clicks and get Nematode
comments.analysis: This comment provides an observation about the game's mechanics, which may be useful for others testing or analyzing the simulation.

comments.author: gregjw
comments.analysis: This comment is a brief, metaphorical statement with little substantive contribution or factual basis.
